hook: Lisbon's legendary no-frills marisqueira where you take a ticket and wait for the city's best seafood.
facts: Founded in 1956; began as a casual beer house before the founder started serving seafood and locals came as much for the marisco as the cerveja.
Family-run institution on Avenida Almirante Reis 1-H, Intendente, open for over six decades.
So busy that guests often take numbered tickets and wait their turn for a table.
Specialises in 'cervejaria-marisqueira' style: prawns, percebes (goose barnacles), crab and a famous prego steak sandwich to finish.
Address: Avenida Almirante Reis 1-H, 1150-007 Lisboa.
legend: Popularised internationally after a glowing on-camera recommendation from the late Anthony Bourdain.

sources.note: Official history page: founded 1956, started as a beer house then seafood.
Apple Maps listing corroborating coordinates 38.7203257, -9.1357084.
Culinary Backstreets: Ramiro as a seafood institution in Intendente.

w: Lisbon's most famous seafood joint, open on Almirante Reis since 1956 and so packed you take a numbered ticket and wait your turn. Tiger prawns, garlicky percebes, crab, and a beef prego to finish — paper tablecloths, no frills, the marisco doing all the talking. Anthony Bourdain put it on the world's map. Get the prawns, then the prego.

hook: The 1905 Chiado café where Fernando Pessoa drank his bica, his bronze likeness now sat at a pavement table outside.
facts: Opened on 19 November 1905 at Rua Garrett 120, Chiado, and is the best-known of Lisbon's historic cafés.
A favourite haunt of writer Fernando Pessoa and a hub of Portuguese intellectual and literary life from the early 20th century.
A bronze statue of Pessoa seated at a table outside was made by sculptor Lagoa Henriques and installed in 1988.
Exterior is Art Nouveau; the interior was redesigned in 1922 with mirrored walls, brass fittings and a long oak bar.
Classified as a Property of Public Interest and one of only a handful of historic Lisbon cafés surviving from the era.

sources.note: Founding 1905, Pessoa statue (Lagoa Henriques, 1988), coordinates 38.7106917, -9.1420472.
Portuguese Wikipedia: history, Rua Garrett 120-122, intellectual gatherings.
Lisbon culture press (A Mensagem) on the café's 117-year history.

w: Fernando Pessoa took his bica at this Chiado café, open since 1905 — and now sits there forever in bronze at a pavement table, sculpted by Lagoa Henriques in 1988. Art Nouveau outside, a mirrored brass-and-oak interior of 1922 within. One of the last of Lisbon's great literary cafés. Pull up a chair beside the poet.

hook: The original ginjinha hole-in-the-wall by Largo de São Domingos, pouring cherry liqueur since 1840.
facts: Opened in 1840 by a Galician named Espinheira; the first shop in Lisbon to sell ginjinha, the city's signature sour-cherry liqueur.
Recipe attributed to advice from a friar of the Igreja de Santo António: ginja cherries fermented in aguardente with sugar, water and cinnamon.
A tiny standing-room counter at Largo de São Domingos 8, just off Rossio, still run by the founding family across generations.
Served by the glass, optionally 'com elas' (with a cherry in the bottom).
Listed under Portugal's 'Lojas com História' / 'Comércio com História' heritage-shop programme.

sources.note: Heritage-shop listing: opened 1840 by Espinheira, first to sell ginjinha.
Portuguese government heritage-commerce listing, Largo de São Domingos.
OpenStreetMap/Nominatim coordinates 38.7146962, -9.1390644.

w: The original ginjinha counter, pouring Lisbon's sour-cherry liqueur since 1840 from a hole in the wall by Largo de São Domingos — the first shop in the city to sell the stuff. The recipe, they say, came from a friar: ginja cherries steeped in aguardente with sugar and cinnamon. A glass, standing, com elas — with a cherry in the bottom.

hook: A 24-seat Graça tavern where amateur fado vadio erupts every weekend afternoon over codfish cakes and red wine.
facts: Small family-run tavern at Rua da Graça 91, in the Graça/São Vicente neighbourhood.
Famous for fado vadio (amateur fado): casual singers take a microphone hanging from the ceiling rather than booked professionals.
Fado happens Saturdays, Sundays and holidays, roughly 4pm to 8pm; only about 24 seats so regulars arrive early.
Drink prices rise once the singing starts, with the difference going to pay the musicians.
Time Out Lisboa lists it among the city's notable fado venues and a genuinely local, non-touristy spot.

sources.note: Time Out: Tasca do Jaime, fado vadio in São Vicente/Graça, Rua da Graça 91.
Local guide on fado vadio at Tasca do Jaime, weekend afternoon sessions.
OpenStreetMap/Nominatim coordinates 38.7191422, -9.1297980.

w: A 24-seat tavern in Graça where fado vadio breaks out every weekend afternoon — no booked professionals, just whoever reaches for the microphone hanging from the ceiling, between codfish cakes and red wine. Drinks cost more once the singing starts; the difference pays the players. Get there early. The realest fado in Lisbon.

hook: A 1927 Baixa wine merchant with a back-room museum of ancient bottles, including Madeira from the 18th century.
facts: Established in 1927 as Mercearia Nacional; specialised in wine through the 1980s, becoming Garrafeira Nacional in 1990.
Still run by descendants of founder Serafim Vaz, now under grandson Jaime Vaz.
Stocks rare and premium Ports, Madeiras, whiskies, cognacs and champagnes.
Houses a small museum of some of the oldest bottles in Portugal, with examples dating back to the 18th century (a 1780 Madeira Boal Borges among them).
Located at Rua de Santa Justa 18, Baixa; listed under 'Lojas com História'.

sources.note: Heritage-shop listing: founded 1927 as Mercearia Nacional, family-run, bottle museum.
Government heritage-commerce listing, Rua de Santa Justa 18.
OpenStreetMap/Nominatim coordinates for Rua de Santa Justa 18: 38.7127268, -9.1372907.

w: A Baixa wine merchant since 1927, still in the founding family — walls of port, madeira, whisky and cognac on Rua de Santa Justa. In the back, a little museum of Portugal's oldest bottles, a 1780 Madeira among them. Where Lisbon comes for the bottle it can't find anywhere else. Liquid history, by the cork.

hook: A riverside warehouse record shop next to Lux, deep in electronica and the home turf of Príncipe's founders.
facts: Founded in 2001 by Pedro Santos and José Moura; one of Lisbon's oldest and most respected record shops.
Occupies a warehouse (Armazém B4, Cais da Pedra) on the Tagus waterfront at Avenida Infante D. Henrique, Santa Apolónia.
Wildly eclectic stock spanning electronica, jazz, krautrock, house, techno, disco, dub, African and Latin styles and more.
Co-founder José Moura is also behind Príncipe Discos, the label that broke Lisbon's Afro-diasporic batida/kuduro scene.
Sits immediately beside the nightclub Lux Frágil; doubles as a venue for concerts, screenings and label events.

sources.note: The Vinyl Factory: founded 2001 by Santos and Moura, Príncipe link, waterfront location.
Official site: address Armazém B4, Cais da Pedra, Santa Apolónia.
Lux Frágil (adjacent landmark) at 38.7150, -9.1206, corroborating the warehouse location just east.

w: A warehouse record shop on the Tagus waterfront beside Lux, dug in since 2001 — electronica, jazz, krautrock, dub, African and Latin, piled deep. Co-founder José Moura also runs Príncipe, the label that took Lisbon's batida and kuduro to the world. Riverside crate-digging, with a club next door. Stay for the night.

hook: An unheated greenhouse-garden built in a former basalt quarry, a lush, dripping hideaway above the Avenida.
facts: Botanic greenhouse-garden in the northwest corner of Parque Eduardo VII, on the site of a 19th-century basalt quarry.
The main 'cold' greenhouse opened in 1933 with the help of painter-architect Raul Carapinha; not artificially heated, hence 'fria'.
Expanded in 1975 with an Estufa Quente (hot) and Estufa Doce (sweet) house for cacti and tropical plants.
Holds species from Australia, Brazil, Korea, Mexico and Peru, including Tasmanian tree ferns, tree rhododendron and camellias.
Roofed by a permeable lath screen that filters light and rain over winding paths, pools and streams.

sources.note: Official Lisbon council site for Estufa Fria: history and the three greenhouses.
Wikipedia: Estufa Fria opened 1933, former quarry, NW corner of Parque Eduardo VII.
OpenStreetMap/Nominatim coordinates 38.7293283, -9.1554966.

w: A greenhouse with no glass and no heat, sunk into an old basalt quarry at the top of Parque Eduardo VII — a slatted roof filters the light and the rain over winding paths, pools and streams. Opened in 1933, later joined by hot and 'sweet' houses for cacti and tropics. Tree ferns, camellias, dripping calm above the Avenida.

hook: A hilltop city of the dead with cypress avenues and the largest private mausoleum in Europe.
facts: Founded in 1833 after a cholera outbreak, alongside the Alto de São João cemetery; laid out in imitation of Paris's Père Lachaise.
Located in the Estrela parish of western Lisbon, covering about 12 hectares; reached by the No. 28 tram terminus.
Home to the Mausoleum of the Dukes of Palmela, considered the largest private mausoleum in Europe, a pyramid over a crypt with capacity for some 200 remains.
The Palmela mausoleum, built in 1848, was designed by Italian architect Giuseppe Cinatti and holds sculpture by Canova, Calmels, Teixeira Lopes and Vítor Bastos.
Lined with stelae, columns and obelisks; the first private mausoleums date from 1839.

sources.note: Wikipedia: founded 1833, Palmela mausoleum, coordinates ~38.7136, -9.1714.
Visit Lisboa: Prazeres as an open-air museum, Palmela mausoleum largest in Europe.
Atlas Obscura entry corroborating significance and location in Estrela.

w: A hilltop city of the dead at the end of the No. 28 tram, laid out in 1833 in the manner of Père Lachaise — cypress avenues lined with stelae, columns and obelisks. At its heart, the 1848 Palmela mausoleum, a stone pyramid said to be the largest private tomb in Europe, with sculpture by Canova. The view over the Tagus is for the living.
